Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, but I made it work. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd block out chunks of time—usually around 18 hours a week—where I could physically work through problems, write things out, and practice. I'd often study at the library or a coffee shop because I don't have reliable internet at home, which meant I had to plan ahead and download everything I needed. Some weeks were more structured than others depending on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and my work schedule. I have extracurricular commitments and sometimes I pick up extra shifts, so I couldn't always stick to the same times each week. When exams approached or big assignments were due, I'd cram in more intensive sessions. Otherwise, I tried to spread things out, but life doesn't always cooperate with ideal study plans.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew there was something specific I needed—like checking an assignment deadline, downloading lecture materials, or seeing if grades were posted. Without internet at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access it, so I'd make trips to campus or places with WiFi specifically for this purpose. Sometimes discussion post deadlines would prompt me to log in too.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a seamless part of my routine because of my internet situation. I had to consciously plan my LMS sessions around when I could get online access. What did become routine was my preparation—I'd make lists of everything I needed to download or check so I could be efficient during my limited online time. It was more of a planned event than a habit.
Q5: The downloadable materials were absolutely essential for me—lecture notes, PDFs, practice problems I could work through by hand. I'm kinesthetic, so anything I could print out and physically interact with was gold. The discussion forums were also helpful when I could participate because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click. Video lectures were useful too, though I had to download them when I could.
Q6: I often couldn't engage much with real-time or synchronous activities because of my internet access issues. Some of the optional readings I'd skip if they weren't directly relevant to assignments or exams—I had to prioritize given my time constraints. The peer interaction features I used minimally, partly because coordinating online was difficult and partly because my anxiety made reaching out to classmates feel overwhelming sometimes.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or to actually understanding the material deeply. If I could physically engage with it—work through problems, take notes, apply concepts—that made it valuable to me. I also valued anything that helped reduce my anxiety about exams, like practice tests or clear study guides.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my high stress levels and limited time, I had to triage constantly. Required activities got done no matter what because my grade depended on it, even if I was exhausted. Optional ones I'd only do if I genuinely felt they'd help me understand something I was struggling with, or if I had a rare moment of breathing room.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could during my LMS sessions, then study from printed materials, handwritten notes, and textbooks at home or wherever. I'd work through problems physically, rewrite concepts, create flashcards—very hands-on stuff. A lot of my learning happened with materials spread across my desk, completely disconnected from any platform.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through downloaded problem sets or re-reading printed lecture notes, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd be at home without internet, deeply engaged with the material, but the LMS would just show silence from me. My exam scores reflected this work, but my online activity definitely didn't capture the full picture.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then study them extensively offline over days or weeks. A single brief login might fuel a whole week of studying. I'd annotate PDFs by hand after printing them, rewatch downloaded videos multiple times—all invisible to the system.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety peaked and everything felt overwhelming. I'd have weeks where competing responsibilities—work, family stuff, extracurriculars—piled up and I just couldn't give the course the attention it deserved. My attendance dropped during those times, and I'd fall behind on assignments even though I was still trying to keep up with the core material.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd prioritize differently—focusing on what would most directly impact my grade rather than engaging broadly with the LMS. Confusion sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I felt. My competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between engaging with course materials or handling something urgent in my life.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about other things, too anxious to really absorb anything. I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't retained anything because my stress was too high. Those sessions felt performative, like I was going through motions without actually learning.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Sometimes my anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd freeze up thinking about everything I needed to do. Other times I was just too exhausted from everything else in my life. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own challenges. My assignment completion rate reflects this struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly the main driver of my LMS usage. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, sometimes in a panic trying to submit things on time. Without deadlines, I might not log in for days because I was studying offline. The deadline pressure was stressful but also necessary—it forced me to prioritize and actually submit work even when my anxiety was telling me it wasn't good enough.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though sometimes not in healthy ways. Positive feedback motivated me to keep pushing, and seeing good assignment scores reminded me I was capable despite my struggles. But waiting for feedback increased my anxiety, and critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid the course for a bit. The CBT sessions I've been doing have helped me process feedback more constructively.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan my limited online time effectively. But the expectation of regular online participation was challenging given my internet situation. I appreciated when materials were downloadable and self-paced, but synchronous expectations or discussion requirements with tight windows were harder for me to meet.
Q19: Downloadable content made engagement much easier—I could work with it on my own terms. Clear assignment rubrics helped reduce my anxiety about what was expected. What made it harder was anything requiring consistent internet access or real-time participation. Also, when course structure was unclear or changed unexpectedly, my anxiety would spike and I'd struggle to adapt.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work—like me sometimes, refreshing to see grades or re-reading instructions because you're worried you missed something. Or maybe they're genuinely trying to understand material but struggling to translate that into finished assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when stress is involved.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS engagement looks low, but I was doing substantial work offline that translated into decent exam scores and assignment quality when I did submit. I think it shows that learning can happen in many ways, and the platform only captures one narrow slice. Someone studying effectively from downloaded materials won't generate much login data.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work—the mechanical aspects of engagement. But they miss the quality of attention, the offline studying, the emotional state of the student. They can't see that I spent four hours with printed notes or that I was too anxious to log in even though I was thinking about the course constantly. They capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm actively trying to understand concepts, connecting them to what I already know, working through problems until they make sense. It's also about caring about the outcome—wanting to learn, not just pass. For me, engagement often happens quietly, with a pen in my hand and papers spread around me, far from any screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept going despite high anxiety and stress, how I found workarounds for my internet limitations, how I used CBT strategies to manage my mental health while still progressing academically. It wouldn't show the hours of offline studying, the handwritten notes, the determination to earn my Merit grade despite everything working against me. The data shows gaps; it doesn't show how hard I fought to close them.
